stories.headline: Iran Ceasefire 'On Verge of Collapse' as Hormuz Mines Remain and Trump Warns of Renewed Strikes
stories.summary:
The fragile two-week ceasefire between the United States and Iran faces mounting pressure, with the Soufan Center think tank warning Thursday that the agreement "hovers on the verge of collapse." Iranian semiofficial news agencies published a chart indicating that the Revolutionary Guard placed sea mines in the Strait of Hormuz's Traffic Separation Scheme during the conflict, raising questions about whether the waterway can be safely reopened. The Strait remains closed to tanker traffic, cutting off a passage through which roughly 20 percent of global oil and natural gas trade once flowed.

President Trump posted on Truth Social Thursday that U.S. warships and troops would remain in the region "until such time as the REAL AGREEMENT reached is fully complied with," adding that renewed military action would be "bigger, and better, and stronger than anyone has ever seen before" if the ceasefire collapses. Trump also reiterated that Iran would not be permitted to develop nuclear weapons and that the Strait of Hormuz "WILL BE OPEN & SAFE."

Adding further instability, Israeli strikes in Lebanon on Wednesday killed and injured hundreds of people, prompting U.N. Secretary-General António Guterres to "unequivocally" condemn the attacks. Analysts noted the strikes risked unraveling the broader ceasefire architecture, as Iran views Lebanese Hezbollah as a key proxy. The Soufan Center wrote that Israel's actions could "be understood both as an effort to drive a wedge between Iran and its proxies and as a response to being allegedly sidelined in the original ceasefire discussions."

Peace talks are scheduled to continue in Islamabad, but the combination of uncleared sea mines, ongoing proxy conflicts in Lebanon, and sharp rhetoric from both Washington and Tehran has left markets and diplomatic observers deeply uncertain about the durability of any agreement.

stories.headline: Melania Trump Denies Epstein Ties in Rare White House Statement, Calls Accusations 'Completely False'
stories.summary:
First Lady Melania Trump delivered an extraordinary statement from the White House Grand Foyer on Thursday, denying any personal ties to the late financier and convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and declaring that "the lies linking me with the disgraceful Jeffrey Epstein need to end today." Reading from a prepared text for approximately five minutes, she described accusations against her as "financially and politically motivated" smears and said she and her attorneys were fighting back against "unfound and baseless lies." She walked away without taking questions after concluding her remarks.

The first lady acknowledged writing a friendly email in October 2002 to Epstein's convicted accomplice Ghislaine Maxwell, signed "Love, Melania," though she did not address the email's content, which reportedly praised a flattering New York magazine profile of Epstein published that week. Melania Trump was also photographed with Epstein at a party at Mar-a-Lago in 2000. She stated Thursday that she was "not Epstein's victim" and had no knowledge of his crimes.

The statement created confusion within the White House over whether President Trump knew about its contents in advance. A spokesperson initially told the New York Times the president was aware she planned to make a statement, then later said it was unclear whether Trump knew the topic. Trump himself told a television host by phone that he did not "know anything about" the statement beforehand, adding simply, "She didn't know him."

Reaction split along familiar lines. Former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene called the statement "brave," while Democrats used it to resurface questions about the administration's handling of the Epstein case. Epstein accusers Maria and Annie Farmer said in a subsequent statement: "What we want is accountability, transparency, and justice." Melania Trump also called on Congress to hold public hearings so survivors could testify and have their accounts entered into the congressional record.
